methodology_sections: 1. Lifecycle Integrity - State transitions, illegal states, predecessors
2. Monotonicity & Irreversibility - Increasing values, one-way transitions
3. Access & Permission Logic - Conditional access, role requirements
4. Time & Resource Integrity - Time-locks, resource bounds, expiry

invariants.description: setReferralFee has no upper bound check, allowing feeManager to set MAX_REFERRAL_FEE > 10000, which causes an arithmetic underflow in Pair._update0/_update1 (amount -= _referralFee where _referralFee > amount), permanently DoSing all swaps on every pair
invariants.attack_scenario: Compromised or malicious feeManager calls setReferralFee(10001). All subsequent swaps revert because in Pair._update0, `amount * 10001 / 10000 > amount`, causing `amount -= _referralFee` to underflow. All trading on all pairs is halted.
invariants.invariant_logic: MAX_REFERRAL_FEE <= 10000 AND for all pairs p: customReferralFees[p] <= 10000

invariants.description: depositsForGenesis calls updateReward(address(0)) instead of updateReward(tokenOwner), so the deposited user's userRewardPerTokenPaid is never set to the current rewardPerTokenStored. This allows genesis depositors to claim rewards earned before their deposit, stealing rewards from other stakers.
invariants.attack_scenario: Alice has 0 balance and userRewardPerTokenPaid=0. Genesis pool deposits 1000 LP on her behalf. The gauge has accumulated rewardPerTokenStored=1e18 from prior epochs. Alice calls getReward() and receives 1000*1e18/1e18 = 1000 reward tokens she was never entitled to, draining rewards from legitimate earlier stakers.
invariants.invariant_logic: For all users u: after depositsForGenesis(u, ..., amount), userRewardPerTokenPaid[u] == rewardPerTokenStored
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_genesisRewardAccounting(address user) public view { if(genesisPool != address(0) && IGenesisPool(genesisPool).balanceOf(user) > 0) { assert(userRewardPerTokenPaid[user] > 0 || rewards[user] == 0); } }

invariants.description: swapExactTokensForTokensSimple does not set routes[0].pair or routes[0].receiver in the route struct (they default to address(0)). getAmountsOut uses routes[i].pair, so for non-concentrated isPair(address(0))=false returns amounts[1]=0 (revert on amountOutMin check). For concentrated, pairToDeployer(address(0)) returns address(0) causing quoter failure. Even if amounts pass, _swap sends tokens to routes[i].receiver=address(0), permanently losing funds.
invariants.attack_scenario: User calls swapExactTokensForTokensSimple with concentrated=false, amountOutMin=0. getAmountsOut returns amounts[1]=0. _swap calls pair.swap with amountOut=0, reverting. Function is completely non-functional. If somehow bypassed (e.g., concentrated swap), tokens go to address(0).
invariants.invariant_logic: For all routes r used in _swap: r.pair != address(0) AND r.receiver != address(0)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_routeFieldsSet(address pair, address receiver) public pure { assert(pair != address(0) && receiver != address(0)); }

invariants.description: _swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens computes amountOutput via getAmountOut which returns the BEST amount across both stable AND volatile pools, but the actual swap is executed on the pool specified by routes[i].stable. When the best pool type differs from the route's stable flag, the calculated output exceeds what the target pool can produce, causing K invariant failure and transaction revert.
invariants.attack_scenario: User routes through stable pair but volatile gives higher output. getAmountOut returns volatile amount. Swap on stable pair with inflated amount fails K check. Alternatively, all FOT swap paths become unreliable depending on pool liquidity distribution. Users cannot reliably use fee-on-transfer token swaps.
invariants.invariant_logic: In _swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens: the pool used for getAmountOut MUST be the same pool used for swap execution
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_fotSwapConsistency() public pure { /* getAmountOut pool type must match routes[i].stable */ assert(false); }

invariants.description: TradeHelper._pairFor computes CREATE2 addresses using PairFactory as deployer, but pairs are actually deployed by PairGenerator via CREATE2. Additionally, pairCodeHash is keccak256(type(Pair).creationCode) which excludes constructor arguments, while CREATE2 uses the full init code including arguments. Both the deployer address and init code hash are wrong, making all computed addresses incorrect.
invariants.attack_scenario: Any contract using TradeHelper.pairFor (e.g., GlobalRouter) computes wrong pair addresses. Swaps routed through GlobalRouter call swap() on non-existent or wrong contracts, causing reverts or potential interaction with malicious contracts deployed at the predicted (wrong) addresses.
invariants.invariant_logic: TradeHelper.pairFor(tokenA, tokenB, stable) == PairFactory.getPair(tokenA, tokenB, stable) for all existing pairs
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_pairForConsistency(address tokenA, address tokenB, bool stable) public view { assert(_pairFor(tokenA, tokenB, stable) == PairFactory(factory).getPair(tokenA, tokenB, stable)); }

invariants.description: getAmountsIn uses a uint loop counter `i` that starts at routes.length-1 and decrements. When i reaches 0, `i--` causes uint underflow to type(uint).max, and the loop condition `i >= 0` is always true for uint256, creating an infinite loop that exhausts all gas.
invariants.attack_scenario: Any call to TradeHelper.getAmountsIn with valid routes will always revert with out-of-gas after the loop processes i=0 and underflows. The function is completely non-functional, breaking reverse-path amount calculations for any integrator.
invariants.invariant_logic: Loop over uint with decrement must use explicit break condition or signed integer, not `i >= 0`
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_getAmountsInTerminates(uint amountOut, route[] memory routes) public view { /* getAmountsIn must not infinite loop */ uint[] memory result = getAmountsIn(amountOut, routes); assert(result.length > 0); }

invariants.description: setInternalBribe has a tautological validation `require(_int >= address(0), 'ZA')` which always passes since all addresses are >= address(0) in uint160. This allows setting internal_bribe to address(0), which would cause all LP fee forwarding to revert when claimFees tries to call notifyRewardAmount on address(0).
invariants.attack_scenario: Owner accidentally or intentionally calls setInternalBribe(address(0)). All subsequent claimFees calls that try to approve and notify address(0) would revert or send fees to a dead address. LP fee distribution is permanently broken until fixed.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_internalBribeNonZero() public view { assert(internal_bribe != address(0)); }

invariants.description: setGenesisStatus does not validate that _pair is a registered pair (no isPair check), unlike setIsGenesis which requires isPair[_pairAddress]. This allows genesisManager or feeManager to set genesis status for arbitrary addresses, potentially blocking swaps on non-pair addresses or bypassing genesis checks.
invariants.attack_scenario: genesisManager calls setGenesisStatus on an arbitrary address. If that address later becomes a pair, it would have unexpected genesis restrictions. Or if router checks isGenesis for routing decisions, the wrong address would affect routing logic.
invariants.invariant_logic: For all addresses a: isGenesis[a] == true implies isPair[a] == true
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_genesisOnlyForPairs(address a) public view { if(isGenesis[a]) { assert(isPair[a]); } }

invariants.description: The _swap function ignores the _to parameter entirely and relies on routes[i].receiver for directing swap outputs. For swapExactTokensForTokens where routes are user-provided calldata, this means the `to` parameter in the public function signature is dead code. Users who set `to` correctly but don't set routes[i].receiver will lose funds to address(0). This breaks the expected API contract.
invariants.attack_scenario: User calls swapExactTokensForTokens with to=userAddress but forgets to set receiver field in routes (defaults to address(0) in memory). Tokens from each swap hop are sent to address(0). The `to` parameter provides false safety since it's never used in _swap.
invariants.invariant_logic: For the last route in a swap: routes[last].receiver == intended_recipient AND routes[last].receiver != address(0)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_swapReceiverSet(address[] memory receivers) public pure { for(uint i=0; i<receivers.length; i++) { assert(receivers[i] != address(0)); } }

invariants.description: The K invariant (_k(balance0, balance1) >= _k(reserve0, reserve1)) must hold after every swap. However, fee distribution sends tokens out of the pair to PairFees and dibs. If referral or staking fees are set to extract too large a portion, the K check could theoretically be gamed. With unbounded referral fees (INV-ACCESS-001), this invariant becomes unreliable.
invariants.attack_scenario: If referral fees are set very close to (but not exceeding) the threshold, most of the swap fee is extracted as referral, leaving minimal incentive for LPs while still passing K check. Combined with high staking fees, LPs receive negligible returns.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_kInvariant() public view { assert(_k(IERC20(token0).balanceOf(address(this)), IERC20(token1).balanceOf(address(this))) >= _k(reserve0, reserve1)); }

invariants.description: emergencyWithdrawAmount has no explicit check that amount <= _balanceOf(msg.sender). While arithmetic underflow in _deductBalance provides implicit protection for _balances, if _totalSupply is manipulated or if genesis pool balance is not properly synced, a user could deduct more from _totalSupply than their actual share, corrupting global accounting.
invariants.attack_scenario: During emergency mode, user calls emergencyWithdrawAmount with amount greater than their actual balance. _deductBalance tries to deduct from _balances[msg.sender] and underflows, reverting. But if genesis pool returns inconsistent balances, the accounting for _totalSupply could become desynced from the sum of all balances.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_emergencyWithdrawBounded(address user, uint256 amount) public view { assert(amount <= _balanceOf(user) && _totalSupply >= amount); }

invariants.description: The fee index system requires _updateFor to be called before any balance change (mint/burn/transfer). If an external integration transfers LP tokens via a mechanism that doesn't trigger _updateFor (e.g., direct storage manipulation in upgradeable proxy, or gauge staking that moves tokens without calling transfer), the fee accounting breaks and allows infinite fee claims.
invariants.attack_scenario: If LP tokens are transferred through a mechanism that bypasses _transferTokens (which calls _updateFor), the recipient's supplyIndex0/1 won't be updated. They can then claim fees accumulated from before they received the tokens, effectively stealing fees from other LP holders.
invariants.invariant_logic: For all balance changes of user u: _updateFor(u) is called BEFORE balanceOf[u] changes
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_feeIndexSync(address user) public view { /* supplyIndex0[user] == index0 whenever balanceOf[user] was last modified */ }

invariants.description: swap is protected by the factory's isPaused flag but mint and burn are NOT. During a pause (intended for emergency), users can still add/remove liquidity but cannot trade. This asymmetry allows LPs to front-run an unpause by adding liquidity during pause when prices may be stale, then extracting value when trading resumes.
invariants.attack_scenario: Protocol detects an exploit and pauses swaps. Attacker adds heavily one-sided liquidity during pause at stale prices. When unpaused, attacker swaps against their own liquidity at favorable rates, extracting value from the pool.
invariants.invariant_logic: If isPaused then mint and burn should also be restricted, OR there must be a mechanism to prevent stale-price exploitation
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_pauseConsistency() public view { if(PairFactory(factory).isPaused()) { /* assert mint/burn are also blocked */ } }

invariants.description: RouterV2 owner is set in constructor with no transfer or renounce mechanism. If the owner's private key is lost or compromised, swapRouter, algebraFactory, quoterV2, and algebraPoolAPIStorage can never be updated (key loss) or can be maliciously changed to drain user funds (key compromise).
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker compromises RouterV2 owner key. Calls setSwapRouter to point to a malicious contract that steals all tokens approved to the router during concentrated liquidity swaps. All users who approved tokens to the router lose funds.
invariants.invariant_logic: owner should have a 2-step transfer mechanism or be a multisig/timelock
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_ownerIsSecure() public view { /* cannot verify programmatically - design invariant */ }

invariants.description: The _withdraw function checks `_balanceOf(msg.sender) > 0` but then withdraws the user-specified `amount` without checking `amount <= _balanceOf(msg.sender)`. Combined with the maturity time check, a user could attempt to withdraw more than their balance if _balanceOf returns a stale or manipulated value from the genesis pool.
invariants.attack_scenario: A user with _balances[user]=100 and genesisPool.balanceOf(user)=100 has _balanceOf=200. User calls _withdraw(200). _deductBalance deducts 100 from genesis and 100 from _balances. But if genesis pool's deductAmount fails to properly reduce the balance, subsequent calls could withdraw more.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_withdrawBounded(address user) public view { /* _totalSupply == sum of all _balanceOf */ }

invariants.description: The maturityTime check in _withdraw (`block.timestamp >= maturityTime[msg.sender]`) only applies to _withdraw, not to emergencyWithdrawAmount. In emergency mode, the maturity time is bypassed. While emergency mode is owner-controlled, this means the maturity lock (intended for genesis pool deposits) can be circumvented by triggering emergency mode.
invariants.attack_scenario: Genesis pool deposits with maturity lock. Owner (or compromised owner) activates emergency mode. All genesis depositors can immediately withdraw, bypassing the maturity time that was part of the genesis pool design. This breaks the time-lock guarantee for token launches.
invariants.invariant_logic: If maturityTime[user] > block.timestamp then user cannot withdraw their genesis deposit tokens, even in emergency
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_maturityEnforced(address user) public view { if(maturityTime[user] > block.timestamp) { /* user's genesis tokens should remain locked */ } }
